'''Denis Charles Betts''' (born 14 September 1969) is an English [[rugby league]] coach and former rugby league footballer. He is the head-coach of [[Wigan Warriors Women]] and a former assistant coach of the [[England national rugby league team|England national team]].

A {{rlp|sr}}, Betts played in the 1980s, 1990s and 2000s for the [[Wigan Warriors]], with whom he won numerous titles, and for the [[New Zealand Warriors|Auckland Warriors]]. He won four caps for England and 32 for [[Great Britain national rugby league team|Great Britain]], setting a record for a forward. He played in two [[Rugby League World Cup]]s, in [[1989–1992 Rugby League World Cup|1989-92]] and [[1995 Rugby League World Cup|1995]], including playing in the finals of both.

==Early life==
Betts was born on 14 September 1969 in [[Salford, Greater Manchester|Salford]], Lancashire and attended Clarendon High School. He has a younger brother, Darren Betts – also a rugby and footballer who played for [[Salford Red Devils|Salford]] in the 1990s. Betts was himself a former [[Manchester United F.C.|Manchester United]] youth team player.

Betts signed for Wigan from Leigh Miners<ref name = "Leighminers">{{Cite web|url=http://www.leighminersrangers.net/openage/?page_id=54|title=Leigh Miners Rangers – About Us|accessdate=25 October 2021|archive-date=24 April 2012|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120424230434/http://www.leighminersrangers.net/openage/?page_id=54|url-status=dead}}</ref> ARL (also known as Leigh Rangers) on 14 October 1986.

==Playing career==
Betts was a part of the Wigan team of the 1980s, and 90's; he won six [[Rugby Football League Championship|Championship]]s, seven Rugby League [[Challenge Cup]]s, three [[Rugby League Premiership|Premiership]]s, four [[League Cup (rugby league)|John Player/Regal Trophies]], and two [[RFL Lancashire Cup|Lancashire Cup]]s. He also received the [[Lance Todd Trophy]] (1991) and the [[Man of Steel Award]] (1995). He featured in three [[World Club Challenge]] matches for Wigan, winning two, against [[Penrith Panthers]] in 1991 and [[Brisbane Broncos]] in 1994.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

In 1995, at the height of the [[Super League War]] he signed a deal with the [[Auckland Warriors]], one of the new clubs, coached by former Wigan coach John Monie.

Later that year Betts became the first player to win both [[England national rugby league team|England]] and [[Great Britain Lions|Great Britain]] caps while not playing in the English leagues. With former Wigan team-mate [[Shaun Edwards]] ruled out by injury, Betts captained England from the second row in the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup final]] at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], but [[Australian Kangaroos|Australia]] won the match 16–8 and retained the [[Rugby League World Cup|World Cup]]. Betts' selection as captain of England meant he was the first player given the honour while not playing in the English leagues.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}}

During the [[1996 Auckland Warriors season]] Betts captained the club. He was selected as vice captain for the [[1996 Great Britain Lions tour]] of Papua New Guinea, Fiji and New Zealand, playing in all Tests against host nations at {{rlp|sr}}. The [[1997 Super League (Australia) season|1997 season]] was Betts' last with the Auckland Warriors as he returned to England to continue playing for Wigan.

Betts returned to play in the [[1998 Wigan Warriors season]] in Europe's [[Super League]] competition, and lost the [[1998 Challenge Cup]] Final to [[Sheffield Eagles]]. He missed Wigan's [[1998 Super League Grand Final]] victory that year through injury. Betts scored Wigan's opening try in the last game at [[Central Park (Wigan)|Central Park]], and their first try at the [[JJB Stadium]].

At the end of 1999's [[Super League IV]], Betts was selected to play for Great Britain in the [[1999 Rugby League Tri-Nations|1999 Tri-Nations]] tournament in both of their matches against New Zealand and Australia.

Betts played for Wigan in their [[2000 Super League Grand Final]] loss to [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]], and their [[2001 Super League Grand Final|2001 Grand Final]] loss to the [[Bradford Bulls]]. He was persuaded to retire from playing at the age of 32, to help Wigan get under the salary cap.<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://menmedia.co.uk/manchestereveningnews/sport/rugby_league/s/182/182081_wigan_blow_as_betts_off.html |title=Wigan blow as Betts off |work=Manchester Evening News |access-date=26 September 2011 |archive-date=12 November 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121112210653/http://menmedia.co.uk/manchestereveningnews/sport/rugby_league/s/182/182081_wigan_blow_as_betts_off.html |url-status=dead }}</ref>

In total, Betts made 32 appearances for Great Britain, the joint highest ever for a forward, and toured three times, in [[1990 Great Britain Lions tour|1990]], [[1992 Great Britain Lions tour of Australia and New Zealand|1992]] and [[1996 Great Britain Lions tour|1996]]. He also captained England in the [[1995 Rugby League World Cup|1995]] [[Rugby League World Cup final|World Cup final]], which they lost to Australia at Wembley in front of 66,540 fans.

==Coaching career==
At the end of 2001, Betts moved into coaching as Under-18s coach at the Wigan Warriors, and then to under-21s, which he led to victory in the 2003 U21 Grand Final.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.wiganwarriorsfans.com/index.php|title=WiganWarriorsFans.com - Index page|website=Wiganwarriorsfans.com}}</ref> In July 2003, [[Stuart Raper]] was terminated as the Wigan Warriors coach, and he was replaced by [[Mike Gregory]]. Gregory appointed Betts as his assistant. The Wigan Warriors went on reach the Grand Final, but lost to [[Bradford Bulls]]. 

The following season, the Wigan Warriors reached final, losing this time to [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] in the [[Challenge Cup]] Final. Betts took temporary charge in May 2004 when Gregory stepped down due to illness. He held the position for a year before he was moved sideways to make way for the appointment of new head coach [[Ian Millward]], who had left [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]]. Betts continued to work under Millward until the end of the 2005's [[Super League X]] but left in November after refusing to take a post as coach of the academy Under-21 team.<ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2006/jan/11/rugbyunion.gdnsport3?INTCMP=SRCH| location=London| newspaper=[[The Guardian]]| first=Ian| last=Malin| title=Rugby union: Gloucester gamble on Betts to help continue revival| date=11 January 2006}}</ref>

In January 2006 he took a position as skills and development coach with [[English Premiership (rugby union)|Guinness Premiership]] [[rugby union]] club [[Gloucester RFC|Gloucester]]. He left during June 2010,<ref>{{cite news| url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_union/my_club/gloucester/8751230.stm| work=BBC News| title=Coach Betts departs Gloucester| date=21 June 2010}}</ref> despite having another year on his contract. Speaking about his time at Gloucester, he said ''"It's a really passionate place and I hope they can get the success they deserve. I really enjoyed the place, especially the match-day atmosphere and the Shed."''<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.thisisgloucestershire.co.uk/Gloucester-Rugby-s-skills-coach-Denis-Betts-hails-soul-Kingsholm/story-11892755-detail/story.html |title=Gloucester Rugby's former skills coach Denis Betts hails the soul of Kingsholm |work=This is Gloucestershire |access-date=27 September 2011 |archive-date=9 June 2012 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120609124745/http://www.thisisgloucestershire.co.uk/Gloucester-Rugby-s-skills-coach-Denis-Betts-hails-soul-Kingsholm/story-11892755-detail/story.html |url-status=dead }}</ref>

While at Gloucester, Betts emerged as a surprise contender for the vacant England coaching job,<ref name="Betts in frame for England job">{{cite web|url=http://www.sportinglife.com/rugbyleague/news/story_get.cgi?STORY_NAME=rleague/10/03/29/RUGBYL_England.html|title=Betts in frame for England job|publisher=Sportinglife|date=29 March 2010|access-date=29 March 2010}}</ref> which he applied for in March 2010, ultimately losing out to [[Bradford Bulls]]' [[Steve McNamara]].

In November 2010, Betts was appointed coach of [[RFL Championship|Championship]] club; the Widnes Vikings.<ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2010/nov/10/denis-betts-widnes-coach| location=London| work=The Guardian| first=Andy| last=Wilson| title=Denis Betts returns as head coach of Widnes| date=10 November 2010}}</ref> In May 2011, he was reappointed to manage the Widnes Vikings into the Super League in 2012.<ref>{{cite news| url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/may/10/denis-betts-widnes-super-league?INTCMP=SRCH| location=London| work=The Guardian| title=Denis Betts to lead Widnes Vikings in Super League| date=10 May 2011}}</ref>

Betts was appointed assistant coach to [[Wayne Bennett (rugby league)|Wayne Bennett]] with England in April 2016. England finished third in the [[2016 Rugby League Four Nations|2016 Four Nations]], and were runners-up in the [[2017 Rugby League World Cup|2017 World Cup]].

In May 2018 the Widnes Vikings terminated Betts' contract with immediate effect due to the Widnes Vikings poor run of winning only three games out of twelve.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/44317074|title = Widnes part company with coach Betts|work = BBC Sport}}</ref>

In October 2023, Betts was announced as head coach of [[Wigan Warriors Women]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://wiganwarriors.com/blog/2023/10/24/betts-appointed-womens-head-coach/|title=Betts appointed Women’s Head Coach|publisher=[[Wigan Warriors]]|date=24 October 2023}}</ref> In his first season, he wore his first piece of silverware as head coach and Wigan's first since 2018 after winning the [[RFL Women's Nines]] competition,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rugby-league.com/article/62994/wigan-warriors-lift-womens-rugby-league-nines-trophy- |title=Wigan Warriors lift Women's Rugby League Nines trophy |website=www.rugby-league.com |date=29 July 2024 |access-date=29 July 2024}}</ref> a trophy the club retained the following year.<ref name=TRL2025>{{cite web|url=https://www.totalrl.com/wigan-warriors-retain-womens-nines-title-in-remarkable-fashion/ |title=Wigan Warriors retain Women's Nines title in remarkable fashion |website=Total Rugby League |date=6 July 2025 |access-date=7 July 2025}}</ref>

Betts first major trophy as head coach came at Wembley in 2025 following Wigan's victory in the [[2025 Women's Challenge Cup final]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/c771ymgvljzo |title=Wigan thrash Saints to win first Women's Challenge Cup |work=BBC Sport |date=7 June 2025 |access-date=12 June 2025}}</ref> Betts's team also performed well in the league that season which saw him named Women's Super League Coach of the Year in 2025,<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/articles/cqje984z4w1o|title=Betts named Women's Super League coach of the year|date=28 September 2025|work=BBC Sport}}</ref> after completing the [[treble (rugby league)|treble]] with Wigan.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/rugby-league/articles/c89dny1jkdwo|title=Women's Super League Grand Final: Wigan Warriors 16-12 St Helens|date=October 5, 2025|website=BBC Sport}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.skysports.com/rugby-league/news/12196/13445531/wigan-warriors-women-16-12-st-helens-women-wigan-fight-back-to-win-womens-super-league-grand-final-and-secure-historic-treble|title=Wigan Warriors Women 16-12 St Helens: Wigan fight back to win Women's Super League Grand Final and secure historic treble|website=Sky Sports}}</ref>
